content.text: Sunan Abī Dāwūd is a collection of ḥadīth compiled by Imām Abū Dāwūd Sulaymān ibn al-Ash'ath as-Sijistānī (raḥimahullāh). It is one of the six canonical collections of ḥadīth (Kutub as-Sittah) and contains 5,274 aḥādīth in 43 books.
Abū Dāwūd Sulaymān ibn al-Ash'ath ibn Isḥāq ibn Bashīr ibn Shaddād ibn 'Amr ibn 'Imrān al-Azdī as-Sijistānī was born in 202 AH in Sijistan, Khurasan, and belonged to the Azd tribe of Yemen.

He began travelling in search of ḥadīth at a young age, reaching Baghdad in 220 AH. His journeys took him through Khurasan, Iraq, Hijaz, Sham, Egypt and Nishapur. He was both a great muḥaddith and an eminent faqīh who studied under Imām Aḥmad ibn Ḥanbal.
Imām an-Nawawī praised his memory, knowledge, intellect, piety and scholarship. Adh-Dhahabī described him as one of the greatest scholars of his era.
A famous incident mentions that he hired a small boat for one dirham just to reply 'Yarhamukallah' to a man who sneezed and said 'Alhamdulillah', hoping for Allah's pleasure.
Imām Abū Dāwūd passed away on Friday, 16 Shawwal 275 AH at the age of 73 and was buried in Basrah.
He studied under nearly 300 scholars including Imām Aḥmad ibn Ḥanbal, Yaḥya ibn Ma'īn, Qutaibah ibn Sa'īd, Abū Bakr ibn Abī Shaibah and many others.
His notable students included Imām at-Tirmidhī, Imām an-Nasā’ī, his son Abū Bakr 'Abdullāh ibn Abī Dāwūd, Ibn Dāsah and many others.
Imām Abū Dāwūd compiled his Sunan mainly around fiqh topics, keeping chapters concise and clearly identifying weak, mursal and munkar narrations whenever necessary.
He stated that he selected about 4,800 narrations from approximately 500,000 aḥādīth.

items: Actions are judged according to intentions.
Part of the beauty of a person's Islam is leaving what does not concern him.
None of you truly believes until he loves for his brother what he loves for himself.
The halal is clear and the haram is clear; avoiding doubtful matters protects one's religion.

content.text: His other famous works include his Letter to the People of Makkah, Masā'il al-Imām Aḥmad, At-Tafarrud, Al-Marāsīl, A'lām an-Nubuwwah, Az-Zuhd and An-Nāsikh wal-Mansūkh.
